CHAPTER 10 — ADVANCED EXCEL FORMULAS, FUNCTIONS, AND TECHNIQUES

An IF statement is the simplest form of branching logic. A condition. A result if the condition is true. A result if it

Chapter 10 — Advanced Excel Formulas, Fu —
NARRATION

An IF statement is the simplest form of branching logic. A condition. A result if the condition is true. A result if it is false.

// SLIDE 02 — WHY LOOKUPS FAIL

WHY LOOKUPS FAIL.

A lookup does one thing: you give it a value, it searches a table, it returns a different value from the same row. Three

Why Lookups Fail — When any of these breaks, you get either a visible error — #N/A, meaning the value was not found — or you get a silent w
NARRATION

A lookup does one thing: you give it a value, it searches a table, it returns a different value from the same row. Three things must be true for this to work correctly. The lookup value must exist in the table. The table must be structured the way the function expects. The match mode must be what you actually want. When any of these breaks, you get either a visible error , #N/A, meaning the value was not found , or you get a silent wrong answer.

// SLIDE 03 — THE THREE LOOKUP FUNCTIONS

THE THREE LOOKUP FUNCTIONS.

—
Lookup_value — Lookup_value controls the lookup_value decision in this workflow.
Table_array — Table_array controls the table_array decision in this workflow.
Col_index_num — Col_index_num controls the col_index_num decision in this workflow.
NARRATION

VLOOKUP searches the leftmost column of a table and returns a value from a column to its right.

// SLIDE 04 — TEXT IS NOT NUMBERS

TEXT IS NOT NUMBERS.

—
CONCAT — CONCAT — key argument to get right applied to the chapter example.
TEXTJOIN — TEXTJOIN — key argument to get right applied to the chapter example.
LEFT/RIGHT/MID — LEFT/RIGHT/MID — key argument to get right applied to the chapter example.
NARRATION

A shipping database has customer names stored as "SMITH, JOHN" in all caps. Your report needs "John Smith." A product code is stored as "US-12345-RED." A downstream system needs just the five-digit number. Dates exported from a legacy system arrive as text strings like "03/15/24" rather than as actual date values. These are text problems, and they appear constantly in real data work. The core principle: Excel stores two kinds of things that look identical.

// SLIDE 05 — DATES ARE NUMBERS

DATES ARE NUMBERS.

—
TODAY/NOW — TODAY/NOW — key argument applied to the chapter example.
DATE — DATE — key argument applied to the chapter example.
DATEDIF — DATEDIF — key argument applied to the chapter example.
NARRATION

Excel stores dates as integers , the number of days since January 1, 1900. The date March 15, 2024 is stored as 45365. When Excel displays it as "3/15/2024," it is applying a format to the underlying number. This matters for date arithmetic: subtracting one date from another gives you the number of days between them, because you are subtracting two integers. If A2 contains a date, this returns the number of days since that date.

// SLIDE 06 — THE VERIFICATION HABIT

THE VERIFICATION HABIT.

—
Known-Good Input Test — Known-Good Input Test — what to test applied to the chapter example.
Missing/Blank Input Test — Missing/Blank Input Test — what to test applied to the chapter example.
Boundary Condition Test — Boundary Condition Test — what to test applied to the chapter example.
NARRATION

Every formula in this chapter can be correct and wrong simultaneously. VLOOKUP with approximate match is correct formula syntax. It produces wrong prices when the table is unsorted. An IFS that checks conditions in the wrong order is correct formula syntax. It misclassifies some records. A TEXT function that formats dates as "mm/dd/yyyy" is correct formula syntax. It produces strings that sort incorrectly. The habit that prevents Friday disasters is verification on edge cases before the formula goes anywhere near real data.

// SLIDE 07 — EXERCISES

EXERCISES

1.→2.→3.→4.→5.
NARRATION

Write an IF formula for column E that returns "PREMIUM" if column C exceeds $500, and "STANDARD" otherwise. Then rewrite it so the threshold is $500 or above (inclusive). What single character changes? Test both with a value of exactly $500 and explain the difference. Tests: the > vs. >= boundary , the off-by-one that matters in pricing and contracts. Difficulty: low. Column A has a product code in the format "US-12345-RED".

// SLIDE 08 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

·Ask it to explain how a modern Excel formula like =VLOOKUP(...) is conceptually a descendant of FLOW-MATIC's verb-noun c
Ask"Hopper coined the term 'computer bug' after finding an actual moth in a relay at Harvard in 1947. The story is famous;
Add the framing"Answer in the rhetorical register of one of Hopper's famous lectures to military or business audiences — direct, story-
NARRATION

The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. Grace Hopper was working on the first compiler (A-0, 1952) and FLOW-MATIC , the moves that let humans write instructions in something close to English and have a machine execute them, which is what every Excel formula descends from decades before most people had heard of spreadsheet formulas and functions. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.
